terms.definition: These are AI systems designed to execute multi-step objectives by independently navigating web browsers, writing code, and iterating on strategies without human intervention. In cybersecurity, these agents use LLMs to analyze software vulnerabilities in real-time and autonomously develop exploits, such as those targeting the Google V8 engine.
terms.analogy: Imagine a self-driving car that doesn't just steer, but also autonomously diagnoses mechanical failures, finds the most efficient shortcut through a construction zone, and negotiates with a toll booth to get the best rate—all without a driver present.
terms.business_value: The shift from AI 'assistants' to 'agents' means your enterprise security perimeter is now facing non-stop, high-speed probing that adapts faster than human response teams. Investing in agentic AI for defense is no longer a luxury; it is a required countermeasure against automated, low-cost offensive exploits.

terms.definition: MoE is an architectural design where a large neural network is divided into specialized sub-networks, or 'experts,' with only a small fraction activated for any single task. Recent advancements allow for 'domain-specific' pruning, where up to 87.5% of these experts are removed to reduce memory footprint while maintaining near-full performance.
terms.analogy: It is like a high-end commercial kitchen staffed with 100 specialist chefs, where the head waiter is smart enough to only wake up the pastry chef and the saucier for a dessert order, keeping the other 98 chefs off the clock to save on payroll.
terms.business_value: This significantly lowers the 'inference tax'—the cost of running AI. Companies can now deploy highly intelligent models on cheaper, memory-constrained hardware or edge devices, transforming AI from a massive capital expenditure into a lean, scalable operational tool.

terms.definition: World modeling is an AI's ability to internalize the laws of physics and logical cause-and-effect rather than simply predicting the next pixel or word in a sequence. While generative models excel at visual 'hallucinations,' true world modeling ensures that objects maintain their mass, gravity acts consistently, and logical continuity is preserved across time.
terms.analogy: Think of a movie set where the backdrop looks incredibly real, but if an actor leans on a 'brick' wall, it wobbles because it is actually painted foam; world modeling is the difference between that Hollywood facade and a building constructed with real steel and stone.
terms.business_value: For sectors like manufacturing, logistics, and robotics, 'stunning' visuals are useless if they are physically impossible. Transitioning to world-model-based AI allows for high-fidelity digital twins and simulations that can accurately predict real-world outcomes, reducing the risk of expensive physical prototyping failures.
